author: @ryanlebeck259
text:
I cast my gears from an old Spirograph into a clay mold and replace my gears using sodium bicarbonate and super glue.

They work well enough for low torque generators and are easy to replace. The machined tolerances of the Spirograph are handy as well for gearing.

author: @21degrees
text: This is a bad idea because of the type of gear surfaces and their shape.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sorry mate - that makes no sense to me - it's a bad idea for long life because the materials are cheap that I can agree with - but because the gear surfaces? and the shape? I don't understand what you mean by that

replies.author: @21degrees
replies.text: I am talking about the mating surfaces and the high friction involved. Recently you did somthing with Drill motor gears and that would be a better solution (in my opinion) respectfully submitted.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: oh for sure - it's badly made - if you want better, basically - pay more - drill motors can't stand much in the way of torque really - they will run a bike or longboard but not a car you would just shear them off - these gears are badly made in this device - that's for sure but a better winch would certainly cope with the torque stresses - so, I am not sure it is a bad idea per se

replies.author: @21degrees
replies.text: Agreed!

author: @totherarf
text:
I believe Volvo used an interesting method of transmission. 
They had two shafts in parallel with a cone on each pointing in opposite directions and a drive belt across the cones!

As you increase torque the band slips to one end of the cones and as you reduce it it goes to the other one end gives low speed high torque the other High speed low torque) ..... not sure you could knock one of them out for £15 ..... but it would also cure your  alignment problem ;o)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I know the method mate - it is awesome for sure

author: @wibblywobblyidiotvision
text: I wouldn't mess about with cheap tat like that.  Used to use the damn things at work, they fall apart really fast if you put any sort of load on them.  And anyway, better stuff can be scrounged up from scrap, which should please you :)  If you can handle a 90° drive, the head of a junked angle grinder generally gets you something like a 4:1 or 6:1 ratio.  Far less lossy, sealed and greased for life.  There's also a pretty solid gear reduction (although not a very high ratio) in corded drills, the bigger the better, or you could look at cordless drills which have a (usually 2 ratio) planetary gearset, although they are less beefy they can be really useful as well.  Circular saws usually have a worm drive setup, which means you can only drive them as a reduction, about 10:1 in most cases, and again, properly beefy.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: good suggestions mate - but I have to say - so much depends - like what if you don't have access to scrap? a lot of folks don't, what about if you just want cheap? what about if you just want fun? what about if you are doing something that doesn't need to last? - like a science fair project - just loads of cases when I can see this being helpful - I am not saying anything against your suggestions - they are awesome - but sometimes cheap and cheerful is good lol - cheers

replies.author: @wibblywobblyidiotvision

author: @401ksolar
text: I had the same issue when designing my wind turbine in the year 2004 I needed to gear it up the option I chose was to use a chain and two sprockets the chain is not extremely tight as the chain lays horizontally however this gave me the gear ratio I needed to get the generator to the speed where it exceeded battery charge voltage level

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome mate

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text: For cheap and easy, I don't think you can beat belt-and-pulley systems.  I'd be willing to bet that a magnetically enhanced belt-pulley would eliminate much of the inherent problems with such a system, as well as being more robust and maintainable in most environments.

replies.author: @401ksolar
replies.text: I tried the belt and pulley system for my wind turbine back in 2003 it was a failure due to the excessive friction that belts cause in their inherent operation it's just physics

replies.author: @azlandpilotcar4450
replies.text: @401ksolar And yet, auto, motorcycle, snowmobile, tractor, (especially CVT) main drives and accessories using belt-and-pulleys in suitably designed and implented systems work fantastically well, and cheaply.  I never said that you could abandon engineering and mechanical expertise, nor that you should quit after a single failure with millions of successes on record.

replies.author: @401ksolar
replies.text: @azlandpilotcar4450 yes I never brought up push belts different subject

replies.author: @401ksolar
replies.text: Also for wind turbines I stand with my statement of experience and observations for decades belts have too much friction in a wind turbine environment

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate
